    "R1.4": "The paper claims SentiBridge is 'the first' cross-lingual sentiment classifier competitive across resource levels, yet directly cites prior work (Liu et al. 2019, Smith et al. 2022) doing overlapping tasks. rationale.R1.2: The text makes a strong 'first of its kind' claim while simultaneously citing Liu et al. (2019) for the same core idea; these must cohere. The logical bridge between 'no translation' novelty and cited prior work doing the same requires consistency checking.
rationale.R1.3: Multiple specific accuracy figures (81.3% vs 78.4% Spanish; 76.1% vs 72.8% Mandarin) are asserted, yet the 'competitive across both high- and low-resource languages' headline claim is only partially supported — Arabic is in training but absent from reported results. Evidence-claim gap is material.
rationale.R1.4: The paper claims SentiBridge is 'the first' cross-lingual sentiment classifier competitive across resource levels, yet directly cites prior work (Liu et al. 2019, Smith et al. 2022) doing overlapping tasks. Whether the novelty delta is real and accurately scoped is central to the delegation.
rationale.R1.7: Smith et al. (2022) is cited as the origin of the training procedure SentiBridge uses, then SentiBridge is said to 'extend' it — the boundary between replication, extension, and novel contribution hinges on accurate attribution. Citation accuracy and coverage directly affect scholarly integrity of the contribution claim.

parsed.clarification_question: Should I prioritize checking whether the empirical results and citations are factually accurate and properly attributed (fact-check / scholarship focus), or whether the logical argument — especially the novelty claim given the cited prior work — holds together internally (consistency / novelty focus)?
